options.content:
CelestiaOps Exoplanet Observatory
Real-time catalog of **confirmed exoplanets** sourced from the NASA Exoplanet Archive (TAP). Data ingested daily at 02:00 UTC. Earth baseline: radius = 1 R⊕, mass = 1 M⊕, orbital period = 365.25 days, equilibrium temperature ≈ 255 K (effective) / 288 K (surface). Habitable zone defined as equilibrium temperature 180 – 310 K.

panels.title: Total Confirmed Exoplanets

targets.rawSql: SELECT COUNT(*) AS value FROM exoplanets

panels.description: Total number of confirmed exoplanets in the NASA archive. Ingested daily. The count crossed 5,000 in 2022 — a milestone that took 27 years from the first confirmed detection in 1992.

panels.title: Habitable Zone Candidates

targets.rawSql: SELECT COUNT(*) AS value FROM exoplanets WHERE is_potentially_habitable = true

panels.description: Planets whose equilibrium temperature falls in the conservative habitable zone (180–310 K). Equilibrium temperature is a theoretical surface temperature assuming Earth-like albedo. It is a necessary but not sufficient condition for liquid water.

panels.title: Sun-like Host Stars (G-type)

targets.rawSql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT hostname) AS value FROM exoplanets WHERE st_teff BETWEEN 5200 AND 6000

panels.description: Host stars with effective temperature between 5,200 K and 6,000 K — the G spectral class, the same as our Sun (5,778 K). G-type stars are relatively stable and long-lived, making them attractive targets in the search for habitable planets.

panels.title: Multi-Planet Systems

targets.rawSql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT hostname) AS value FROM exoplanets WHERE sy_pnum >= 2

panels.description: Star systems with two or more confirmed planets. Multi-planet systems are important because they suggest dynamically stable architectures and provide comparative context — planets in the same system formed from the same protoplanetary disk.

panels.title: Nearest Exoplanet (ly)

targets.rawSql: SELECT ROUND((MIN(sy_dist) * 3.26156)::numeric, 2) AS value FROM exoplanets WHERE sy_dist IS NOT NULL

panels.description: Distance in light-years to the closest confirmed exoplanet. Currently Proxima Centauri d at 4.24 ly. For scale: the New Horizons spacecraft, the fastest object humans have launched, would take approximately 78,000 years to reach it.

panels.title: Last Ingest

targets.rawSql: SELECT TO_CHAR(last_sync_at AT TIME ZONE 'UTC', 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI') AS value FROM sync_state WHERE dag_id = 'ingest_exoplanets'

panels.description: Timestamp of the last successful NASA TAP ingest run. The pipeline runs daily at 02:00 UTC. If this timestamp is more than 36 hours old the snapshot DAG will refuse to run until the data is refreshed.

panels.title: Planet Classification by Radius
panels.description: Rocky < 1.5 R⊕ | Super-Earth 1.5–2.5 | Sub-Neptune 2.5–4 | Neptune-like 4–10 | Gas Giant > 10

targets.rawSql: SELECT COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE pl_rade < 1.5) AS "Rocky (<1.5 R⊕)", COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE pl_rade >= 1.5 AND pl_rade < 2.5) AS "Super-Earth (1.5-2.5)", COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE pl_rade >= 2.5 AND pl_rade < 4.0) AS "Sub-Neptune (2.5-4)", COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE pl_rade >= 4.0 AND pl_rade < 10.0) AS "Neptune-like (4-10)", COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE pl_rade >= 10.0) AS "Gas Giant (>10)" FROM exoplanets WHERE pl_rade IS NOT NULL

SELECT COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE st_teff < 3700) AS "M — Red Dwarf (<3700 K)", COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE st_teff >= 3700 AND st_teff < 5200) AS "K — Orange (3700-5200 K)", COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE st_teff >= 5200 AND st_teff < 6000) AS "G — Sun-like (5200-6000 K)", COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE st_teff >= 6000 AND st_teff < 7500) AS "F — Yellow-White (6000-7500 K)", COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE st_teff >= 7500) AS "A+ — Hot (>7500 K)" FROM exoplanets WHERE st_teff IS NOT NULL

panels.title: Discovery Methods

targets.rawSql: SELECT COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE discoverymethod = 'Transit') AS "Transit", COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE discoverymethod = 'Radial Velocity') AS "Radial Velocity", COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE discoverymethod = 'Microlensing') AS "Microlensing", COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE discoverymethod = 'Imaging') AS "Imaging", COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE discoverymethod = 'Transit Timing Variations') AS "Transit Timing Variations", COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE discoverymethod NOT IN ('Transit','Radial Velocity','Microlensing','Imaging','Transit Timing Variations')) AS "Other" FROM exoplanets WHERE discoverymethod IS NOT NULL

panels.description: How each planet was detected. Transit (74%) dominates because Kepler and TESS observed hundreds of thousands of stars simultaneously. Radial Velocity was the primary method before space telescopes. Microlensing and Imaging are rarer but can detect planets that transits cannot.

panels.title: Annual Discoveries

targets.rawSql: SELECT disc_year::text AS "Year", COUNT(*) AS "New Planets Confirmed" FROM exoplanets WHERE disc_year IS NOT NULL GROUP BY disc_year ORDER BY disc_year

panels.description: Confirmed discoveries per year. The spike around 2014–2016 is Kepler's statistical validation wave — hundreds of candidates confirmed simultaneously using probability-based methods. Post-2020 growth is predominantly from TESS.

panels.title: Cumulative Exoplanet Count Over Time

targets.rawSql: SELECT make_date(disc_year::int, 6, 1) AS "time", SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER (ORDER BY disc_year) AS "Cumulative Planets" FROM exoplanets WHERE disc_year IS NOT NULL GROUP BY disc_year ORDER BY disc_year

panels.description: Running total of confirmed planets over time. The curve is accelerating — the first 1,000 confirmations took 17 years (1992–2009); the most recent 1,000 took under two years. Space-based transit surveys fundamentally changed the rate of discovery.

panels.title: Planet Catalog — All Confirmed Exoplanets

targets.rawSql: SELECT pl_name AS "Planet", hostname AS "Host Star", disc_year AS "Year", discoverymethod AS "Method", ROUND(pl_rade::numeric, 2) AS "Radius (R⊕)", ROUND(pl_masse::numeric, 2) AS "Mass (M⊕)", ROUND(pl_orbper::numeric, 2) AS "Period (days)", ROUND(pl_eqt::numeric, 0) AS "Eq. Temp (K)", ROUND(sy_dist::numeric, 1) AS "Dist (pc)", ROUND((sy_dist * 3.26156)::numeric, 1) AS "Dist (ly)", sy_pnum AS "# Planets in System", CASE WHEN is_potentially_habitable THEN 'HZ' ELSE '' END AS "HZ" FROM exoplanets ORDER BY disc_year DESC, pl_name LIMIT 2000

panels.description: Full searchable catalog. All columns are sortable. The HZ column (green badge) marks habitable zone candidates. The Eq. Temp column is color-coded: blue = frozen, green = habitable zone, orange = hot, red = extreme. Dist (ly) = distance in light-years (1 parsec = 3.26 ly). R⊕ and M⊕ are Earth-relative units.

panels.title: 🌱 Habitable Zone Candidates — Ordered by Earth Temperature Similarity
panels.description: Planets with equilibrium temperature 180–310 K, closest to Earth's ~288 K surface average

targets.rawSql: SELECT pl_name AS "Planet", hostname AS "Star", ROUND(pl_rade::numeric, 2) AS "Radius (R⊕)", ROUND(pl_masse::numeric, 2) AS "Mass (M⊕)", ROUND(pl_eqt::numeric, 0) AS "Eq. Temp (K)", ROUND((pl_eqt - 288)::numeric, 0) AS "ΔT vs Earth (K)", ROUND(sy_dist::numeric, 1) AS "Dist (pc)", ROUND((sy_dist * 3.26156)::numeric, 1) AS "Dist (ly)", disc_year AS "Year", discoverymethod AS "Method" FROM exoplanets WHERE is_potentially_habitable = true ORDER BY ABS(COALESCE(pl_eqt, 9999) - 288), pl_name

panels.title: Nearest Exoplanets to Earth

targets.rawSql: SELECT pl_name AS "Planet", hostname AS "Star", ROUND((sy_dist * 3.26156)::numeric, 3) AS "Distance (ly)", ROUND(sy_dist::numeric, 3) AS "Distance (pc)", ROUND(pl_rade::numeric, 2) AS "Radius (R⊕)", ROUND(pl_masse::numeric, 2) AS "Mass (M⊕)", ROUND(pl_eqt::numeric, 0) AS "Eq. Temp (K)", disc_year AS "Year", CASE WHEN is_potentially_habitable THEN 'HZ' ELSE '' END AS "HZ" FROM exoplanets WHERE sy_dist IS NOT NULL ORDER BY sy_dist ASC LIMIT 30

panels.description: The 30 nearest confirmed exoplanets by distance. Distance color: green = within 50 ly, yellow = 50–200 ly, orange = 200–1000 ly, red = beyond. HZ badge indicates habitable zone candidates. Proxima Cen b and d are the closest at 4.24 ly.

panels.title: Planet Count by Size Class (Radius Bins)
panels.description: Rocky < 1.5 R⊕ | Super-Earth 1.5–2.5 | Sub-Neptune 2.5–4 | Neptune-like 4–10 | Gas Giant > 10. Earth = 1 R⊕, Neptune = 3.9 R⊕, Jupiter = 11.2 R⊕

targets.rawSql:
SELECT CASE WHEN pl_rade < 1.5 THEN '1. Rocky
<1.5 R⊕' WHEN pl_rade < 2.5 THEN '2. Super-Earth
1.5–2.5 R⊕' WHEN pl_rade < 4.0 THEN '3. Sub-Neptune
2.5–4 R⊕' WHEN pl_rade < 10.0 THEN '4. Neptune-like
4–10 R⊕' ELSE '5. Gas Giant
>10 R⊕' END AS "Class", COUNT(*) AS "Planets" FROM exoplanets WHERE pl_rade IS NOT NULL GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1

panels.title: 🌌 Most Prolific Multi-Planet Systems

targets.rawSql: SELECT hostname AS "Star System", MAX(sy_pnum) AS "Planets", COUNT(*) AS "Confirmed", MIN(disc_year) AS "First Disc.", ROUND(AVG(pl_orbper)::numeric, 1) AS "Avg Period (days)", ROUND(AVG(pl_rade)::numeric, 2) AS "Avg Radius (R⊕)", ROUND(MIN(sy_dist * 3.26156)::numeric, 1) AS "Dist (ly)" FROM exoplanets WHERE sy_pnum >= 3 GROUP BY hostname, sy_pnum ORDER BY sy_pnum DESC, hostname LIMIT 30

panels.description: Star systems with 3 or more confirmed planets, ranked by planet count. KOI-351 (Kepler-90) has 8 confirmed planets — the only known system matching our Solar System. TRAPPIST-1 has 7, all rocky and several in the habitable zone. Planets column is color-coded: blue = 3, green = 4+, yellow = 6+, orange = 8+.

panels.title: Orbital Period Distribution (days < 1000)
panels.description: Earth = 365.25 days. Most close-in transiting planets have periods < 10 days.

targets.rawSql: SELECT CASE WHEN pl_orbper < 2 THEN '0–2d (Hot)' WHEN pl_orbper < 10 THEN '2–10d' WHEN pl_orbper < 30 THEN '10–30d' WHEN pl_orbper < 100 THEN '30–100d' WHEN pl_orbper < 200 THEN '100–200d' WHEN pl_orbper < 365 THEN '200–365d' WHEN pl_orbper < 500 THEN '365–500d (Earth-like)' ELSE '500–1000d' END AS "Period Range", COUNT(*) AS "Planets" FROM exoplanets WHERE pl_orbper BETWEEN 0.1 AND 1000 GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY MIN(pl_orbper)

panels.title: Equilibrium Temperature Distribution
panels.description: Planet count by equilibrium temperature bucket. The 180–310 K range is the conservative habitable zone (HZ) — where liquid water could exist on the surface. Earth's effective temperature is ~255 K. Note: all bars share the same color; the x-axis labels identify each bucket.

targets.rawSql: SELECT CASE WHEN pl_eqt < 180 THEN '<180K (Frozen)' WHEN pl_eqt < 310 THEN '180-310K (Habitable Zone)' WHEN pl_eqt < 500 THEN '310-500K (Hot)' WHEN pl_eqt < 1000 THEN '500-1000K (Very Hot)' WHEN pl_eqt < 2000 THEN '1000-2000K (Lava)' ELSE '>2000K (Ultra-Hot)' END AS "Temp Range", COUNT(*) AS "Planets" FROM exoplanets WHERE pl_eqt IS NOT NULL GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY MIN(pl_eqt)

panels.title: Distance Distribution (parsecs < 3000)
panels.description: 1 parsec = 3.26 light-years. Kepler field ≈ 300–3000 pc.

targets.rawSql: SELECT CASE WHEN sy_dist < 10 THEN '<10 pc (33 ly)' WHEN sy_dist < 50 THEN '10–50 pc' WHEN sy_dist < 100 THEN '50–100 pc' WHEN sy_dist < 300 THEN '100–300 pc' WHEN sy_dist < 500 THEN '300–500 pc' WHEN sy_dist < 1000 THEN '500–1000 pc' WHEN sy_dist < 2000 THEN '1000–2000 pc' ELSE '2000–3000 pc' END AS "Distance Range", COUNT(*) AS "Planets" FROM exoplanets WHERE sy_dist IS NOT NULL AND sy_dist < 3000 GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY MIN(sy_dist)

panels.title: Hottest Planets (Highest Eq. Temperature)

targets.rawSql: SELECT pl_name AS "Planet", hostname AS "Star", ROUND(pl_eqt::numeric, 0) AS "Eq. Temp (K)", ROUND((pl_eqt - 273.15)::numeric, 0) AS "Temp (°C)", ROUND(pl_orbper::numeric, 3) AS "Period (days)", ROUND(pl_rade::numeric, 2) AS "Radius (R⊕)", disc_year AS "Year" FROM exoplanets WHERE pl_eqt IS NOT NULL ORDER BY pl_eqt DESC LIMIT 20

panels.description: Planets ranked by equilibrium temperature. KELT-9 b tops the list at 4,050 K — hotter than many stars, hot enough to vaporize iron. These are tidally locked hot Jupiters orbiting within 0.05 AU of their host star. Temperature color: orange = hot, red = above 2,000 K, dark red = above 3,000 K.

panels.title: Largest Planets by Radius

targets.rawSql: SELECT pl_name AS "Planet", hostname AS "Star", ROUND(pl_rade::numeric, 2) AS "Radius (R⊕)", ROUND((pl_rade / 11.209)::numeric, 3) AS "Radius (R♃)", ROUND(pl_masse::numeric, 1) AS "Mass (M⊕)", ROUND(pl_orbper::numeric, 2) AS "Period (days)", disc_year AS "Year" FROM exoplanets WHERE pl_rade IS NOT NULL ORDER BY pl_rade DESC LIMIT 20

panels.description: Planets ranked by radius in Earth radii (R⊕) and Jupiter radii (R♃, where 1 R♃ = 11.209 R⊕). Values above ~20 R⊕ are likely inflated hot Jupiters — stellar irradiation causes their atmospheres to expand significantly beyond what their mass alone would predict. Color: blue = Neptune-scale, purple = Jupiter-scale, red = inflated giant.

panels.title: Most Massive Planets

targets.rawSql: SELECT pl_name AS "Planet", hostname AS "Star", ROUND(pl_masse::numeric, 1) AS "Mass (M⊕)", ROUND((pl_masse / 317.83)::numeric, 3) AS "Mass (M♃)", ROUND(pl_rade::numeric, 2) AS "Radius (R⊕)", ROUND(pl_orbper::numeric, 2) AS "Period (days)", disc_year AS "Year" FROM exoplanets WHERE pl_masse IS NOT NULL ORDER BY pl_masse DESC LIMIT 20

panels.description: Planets ranked by mass in Earth masses (M⊕) and Jupiter masses (M♃, where 1 M♃ = 317.83 M⊕). Above ~13 M♃ objects are typically classified as brown dwarfs rather than planets. Mass is only measurable via radial velocity or transit timing — roughly 53% of confirmed planets have no measured mass, which is why this table is shorter than the radius table.

panels.title: Longest Orbital Periods — The Cold Outer Worlds
panels.description: For reference: Earth = 365.25 d, Jupiter = 4333 d (11.9 yr), Neptune = 60,190 d (164.8 yr)

targets.rawSql: SELECT pl_name AS "Planet", hostname AS "Star", ROUND(pl_orbper::numeric, 1) AS "Period (days)", ROUND((pl_orbper / 365.25)::numeric, 2) AS "Period (years)", ROUND(pl_rade::numeric, 2) AS "Radius (R⊕)", ROUND(pl_masse::numeric, 1) AS "Mass (M⊕)", ROUND(pl_orbsmax::numeric, 3) AS "Semi-major Axis (AU)", discoverymethod AS "Method", disc_year AS "Year" FROM exoplanets WHERE pl_orbper IS NOT NULL ORDER BY pl_orbper DESC LIMIT 20

panels.title: Coolest Stars Hosting Exoplanets (M-dwarfs)
panels.description: Red dwarfs (< 3700 K) are the most common stars and frequently host rocky planets in tight orbits

targets.rawSql: SELECT hostname AS "Star", ROUND(MIN(st_teff)::numeric, 0) AS "Star Temp (K)", ROUND(MIN(st_rad)::numeric, 3) AS "Star Radius (R☉)", ROUND(MIN(st_mass)::numeric, 3) AS "Star Mass (M☉)", MAX(sy_pnum) AS "Planets in System", ROUND(MIN(sy_dist * 3.26156)::numeric, 2) AS "Dist (ly)", MIN(disc_year) AS "First Disc." FROM exoplanets WHERE st_teff < 3700 AND st_teff IS NOT NULL GROUP BY hostname ORDER BY MIN(st_teff) ASC LIMIT 25
